items.title: Document the workflow in `CONTRIBUTING.md`
items.avoid: Implicit tribal knowledge ('we usually do X but...').
items.prefer: A 1-page file: which branches exist, how to name them, what the merge button should be, who approves.
items.why: New hires onboard faster; you stop re-litigating the same arguments per PR.
items.example: Include: default branch, branch-name regex, merge strategy, who reviews, release process.

items.title: Default to the simplest workflow that works
items.prefer: Start with GitHub Flow / trunk-based; add structure only when a real pain point demands it.
items.why: Process you don't need is friction. Earn complexity.
items.example: Need staged rollouts? Add environment branches. Multiple supported versions? Add release branches. Not yet? Don't.

items.title: Use feature flags for trunk-based
items.avoid: Long-lived feature branches because you 'can't merge yet'.
items.prefer: Merge incrementally behind a flag (`if cfg.experimental.foo:`).
items.why: Lets the team integrate continuously without exposing unfinished work to users.

items.title: Protect `main` (and other important branches)
items.avoid: Direct pushes to `main` and no required reviews.
items.prefer: Branch protection: require PRs, ≥1 review, green CI, no force-pushes.
items.why: Process you can configure is process that gets followed; manual discipline isn't enough at any team size.
items.example: GitHub branch protection: 'Require pull request', 'Require status checks', 'Include administrators'.

items.title: Tag every release with SemVer (or CalVer)
items.avoid: Ad-hoc release names, undated tags, missing tags.
items.why: Tags are the immutable anchors for rollbacks, eval reports and changelogs.
items.example:
```bash
git tag -a v1.2.3 -m "Release 1.2.3"
git push origin v1.2.3
```
